content.text: Scheduled Upgrade Windows and Rollback Plans

Dependency upgrades are not deploy-at-any-time operations. An upgrade to the Anthropic SDK or a container base image touches the production environment in ways that can affect availability, latency, and model call behaviour. Scheduling upgrades in dedicated windows — with baseline metrics captured, rollback artifacts staged, and on-call coverage confirmed — converts what could be an ad hoc risk into a controlled operation. The window structure also creates a natural forcing function: if an upgrade cannot be prepared and tested within the pre-window period, it is deferred to the next window rather than rushed.

attrs.alt: Upgrade window lifecycle: four phases — Pre-Window (T-48h), Deploy (T+0 to T+30m), Monitor (T+30m to T+90m), Close (T+90m) — with rollback path at bottom

content.text: An upgrade window is a time-bounded operation with four phases: pre-window preparation, deployment execution, monitoring, and closure. Each phase has entry criteria (what must be true before it starts) and exit criteria (what must be true before the next phase begins). The window's total duration — typically 90 minutes for a standard dependency bump — is chosen to fit within a business-hours period when the full on-call team is available, while still being short enough to minimise the deployment freeze on other work.

The is_overdue method matters in practice: if a deploy is still in the DEPLOY or MONITOR phase when the window_end timestamp passes, the on-call engineer must make an explicit decision — extend the window (with updated notifications), roll back, or mark the window as closed anyway with a note. Silently running past the end of the window is not acceptable because it means the freeze on other production deploys extends without clear communication to the team.

The pre-window period (typically starting 48 hours before the window) is where most upgrade failures are caught before they reach production. A dependency bump that breaks the test suite in staging is far cheaper to handle during pre-window preparation than during the deploy phase when other engineers are standing by and the production freeze is active.

Deploy the dependency upgrade to staging at least 24 hours before the production window. This gives time to catch slow-manifesting regressions (memory leaks, connection pool exhaustion) that would not appear in a 30-minute test run.

Record current production error rate, P99 latency, and health check response time. These values become the rollback triggers during the monitoring phase. Capturing them immediately before the window (not hours before) ensures the comparison is against recent traffic patterns, not a stale snapshot.

Build and tag the previous version's container image and push it to the registry. The rollback command must be a single operation (a kubectl set image or a docker service update) that the on-call engineer can execute in under 2 minutes. Never rely on being able to rebuild the previous version during a rollback — build systems fail at the worst times.

Confirm that the on-call engineer knows the window is scheduled, knows the rollback procedure, and has the rollback command ready to execute. An upgrade window where the on-call engineer learns about it when it starts is a window with no effective rollback capability.

Announce the production freeze window in the engineering channel. Other engineers should not be mid-deploy of unrelated changes when the upgrade window opens — a concurrent deploy creates ambiguity about which change caused any regression that appears.

The rollback plan is written before the window opens, not improvised during a regression. It specifies the exact commands to execute, the expected outcomes of each command, and the time budget for the rollback to complete before the situation escalates from "possible regression" to "confirmed incident." A rollback plan that requires judgment calls during execution is not a rollback plan — it is a set of notes.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import datetime
from enum import Enum

class RollbackReason(str, Enum):
    ERROR_RATE_SPIKE  = "error_rate_spike"
    LATENCY_SPIKE     = "latency_spike"
    HEALTH_CHECK_FAIL = "health_check_fail"
    MANUAL_DECISION   = "manual_decision"

@dataclass
class RollbackPlan:
    component: str
    current_version: str          # version being deployed (to roll back from)
    previous_version: str         # version to roll back to
    rollback_image_tag: str       # docker image tag of previous version
    rollback_command: str         # exact command — no placeholders
    expected_time_to_healthy_minutes: int = 5

    # Trigger thresholds (compared against baseline captured pre-window)
    error_rate_trigger_multiplier: float = 2.0
    latency_p99_trigger_multiplier: float = 3.0

    def should_rollback(
        self,
        current_error_rate: float,
        baseline_error_rate: float,
        current_p99_ms: float,
        baseline_p99_ms: float,
        reason: RollbackReason | None = None,
    ) -> tuple[bool, str]:
        if reason in (RollbackReason.HEALTH_CHECK_FAIL, RollbackReason.MANUAL_DECISION):
            return True, reason.value
        if current_error_rate > baseline_error_rate * self.error_rate_trigger_multiplier:
            return True, (
                f"error_rate {current_error_rate:.3f} "
                f"exceeds {self.error_rate_trigger_multiplier}x "
                f"baseline {baseline_error_rate:.3f}"
            )
        if current_p99_ms > baseline_p99_ms * self.latency_p99_trigger_multiplier:
            return True, (
                f"p99 {current_p99_ms:.0f}ms "
                f"exceeds {self.latency_p99_trigger_multiplier}x "
                f"baseline {baseline_p99_ms:.0f}ms"
            )
        return False, "within_thresholds"

    def execute_record(
        self,
        reason: str,
        initiated_by: str,
        initiated_at: datetime,
    ) -> dict:
        return {
            "component": self.component,
            "rolled_back_from": self.current_version,
            "rolled_back_to": self.previous_version,
            "reason": reason,
            "command_executed": self.rollback_command,
            "initiated_by": initiated_by,
            "initiated_at": initiated_at.isoformat(),
            "note": (
                f"Dependency upgrade of {self.component} rolled back. "
                f"Previous version {self.previous_version} restored. "
                f"CVE status: re-open if patch was for a security finding."
            ),
        }
Choosing a Deployment Strategy for Upgrades

Most dependency upgrades can use a rolling deployment: instances are updated one group at a time, with health checks between groups. Traffic shifts gradually to updated instances as they become healthy. Rolling deployments have near-zero downtime and allow automatic rollback at any point in the rollout if a health check fails. They are the default for patch and minor version upgrades where breaking changes are not expected.
Patch and minor version bumps; no breaking changes
Major version bumps; database schema or config changes
Zero (blue stays live until green is verified)
SDK upgrades with uncertain output impact; high-risk changes
Container base image updates requiring full restart

The canary strategy is particularly well-suited to Anthropic SDK upgrades because it allows the new SDK version to serve a small percentage of real traffic while the team monitors for output quality changes. A structural regression in the inference path — a streaming parse change, a changed default stop sequence — will appear in the canary's error logs or golden-set monitoring before it affects the full user base. Five percent of traffic is enough to detect systematic regressions while limiting customer exposure.

Upgrade windows should occur on a predictable cadence so the team internalises them as routine operations, not emergency events. The cadence for routine dependency upgrades is typically biweekly (every two weeks) or monthly. Security patches with a 72-hour deadline need a shorter path: a pre-approved emergency window that can be opened without waiting for the next scheduled slot.

The fits_in_recurring_slot method drives the scheduling decision: if the next recurring Tuesday slot falls before the latest acceptable date for this upgrade's track, it can go into the normal queue. If it doesn't fit — a prioritise-track item with a 7-day deadline where the next Tuesday is 8 days away — the window must be scheduled earlier, either in the Thursday slot or as a special session. The emergency slot bypasses the normal queue entirely and requires incident commander approval to open.

Every upgrade window closes with a post-upgrade review ticket filed within the window closure phase. The review is conducted within 7 days of the window close and covers four questions: did the deploy proceed as planned, did the monitoring window show any unexpected behaviour, are the rollback triggers calibrated correctly for this component, and what would improve the next upgrade of this dependency. The review is short — 30 minutes — but its output feeds back into the upgrade plan for the next version bump.
Identify bottleneck; extend default window duration
Document root cause; update pre-window test checklist
Rebuild rollback artifact; rehearse command in staging
Patch compliance record reflects new version + window date
Update record immediately; compliance gap otherwise
Batching Multiple Upgrades in One Window

Each upgrade window carries a fixed cost: pre-window preparation, on-call coverage, monitoring, and post-review. Batching multiple small dependency upgrades into a single window amortises this cost, but introduces a new risk: when something goes wrong during a multi-dependency window, diagnosing which upgrade caused the regression is harder than isolating a single-change deploy. The batching strategy balances efficiency against debuggability.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum

class BatchSafety(str, Enum):
    SAFE      = "safe"      # safe to batch with others
    ISOLATED  = "isolated"  # must deploy alone; too risky to batch
    SEQUENTIAL = "sequential"  # batch OK but must deploy before others in batch

@dataclass
class UpgradeBatchRule:
    dependency_name: str
    safety: BatchSafety
    reason: str

BATCH_RULES = [
    UpgradeBatchRule(
        dependency_name="anthropic-sdk",
        safety=BatchSafety.ISOLATED,
        reason=(
            "SDK is on the inference call path. A regression during a "
            "multi-dep window creates ambiguity about the cause. "
            "Always deploy SDK upgrades alone."
        ),
    ),
    UpgradeBatchRule(
        dependency_name="container-base-image",
        safety=BatchSafety.ISOLATED,
        reason=(
            "Base image update changes the entire runtime environment. "
            "Batch regression diagnosis requires knowing which library changed."
        ),
    ),
    UpgradeBatchRule(
        dependency_name="auth-library",
        safety=BatchSafety.SEQUENTIAL,
        reason=(
            "Auth library must deploy and stabilise before other changes "
            "to avoid conflating auth failures with other regressions."
        ),
    ),
]

def can_batch(names: list[str]) -> tuple[bool, str]:
    """
    Returns (ok, reason). False if any dep in the batch has ISOLATED safety,
    or if more than one dep has SEQUENTIAL safety.
    """
    rules_by_name = {r.dependency_name: r for r in BATCH_RULES}
    isolated = [n for n in names if rules_by_name.get(n, UpgradeBatchRule(n, BatchSafety.SAFE, "")).safety == BatchSafety.ISOLATED]
    sequential = [n for n in names if rules_by_name.get(n, UpgradeBatchRule(n, BatchSafety.SAFE, "")).safety == BatchSafety.SEQUENTIAL]

    if isolated:
        return False, f"{', '.join(isolated)} must deploy in isolated windows"
    if len(sequential) > 1:
        return False, f"Multiple sequential deps in one batch: {', '.join(sequential)}"
    return True, "batch is safe"

In practice, a biweekly upgrade window typically batches 3-8 utility and framework dependencies together (logging, date utilities, testing libraries, minor HTTP client bumps) while SDK upgrades and base image updates always run in their own dedicated windows. This split gives the efficiency benefits of batching for the long tail of low-risk upgrades while preserving isolation for the changes most likely to introduce difficult-to-diagnose regressions.

The rollback trigger thresholds (2x error rate, 3x P99 latency) are defaults, not universal values. Applications with naturally high variance in traffic patterns need looser thresholds to avoid false rollback triggers on normal traffic spikes. Applications with very tight SLAs may need tighter thresholds to catch regressions before they become SLA breaches. Threshold calibration is part of the pre-window preparation for each dependency category.
Any regression in inference path is significant; tight triggers
Standard; transient API errors can create spike without regression
Latency variance is higher on restart; looser P99 trigger
Low risk; looser thresholds reduce false rollback triggers

Threshold calibration must be reviewed after every rollback. If a rollback was triggered by a false positive (a traffic spike that would have resolved naturally, not a regression), the threshold was too tight. If a regression reached 30% of users before the rollback triggered, the threshold was too loose. Post-rollback review always includes a threshold review as one of its four agenda items.

Enterprise customers often have maintenance window preferences specified in their support agreements: preferred hours (business hours in their timezone), blackout periods (no deploys during end-of-quarter financial close, product launches, or major events), and advance notice requirements. Managing upgrade windows for a multi-customer API requires awareness of these preferences even when the deploy does not cause downtime — a rolling deploy that introduces a 200ms latency increase for 30 minutes during a customer's peak load window is a support event even without an outage.

The compliance_issues method is called during the pre-window preparation phase for every enterprise customer with a maintenance agreement. If the list is non-empty, the window is either rescheduled or the account executive is notified to manage the customer relationship proactively. A compliance issue discovered after the window is already in progress is far harder to address than one caught 48 hours before.

Scheduled upgrade windows convert dependency upgrades from ad hoc deploys into controlled operations with defined phases, entry and exit criteria, and pre-staged rollback artifacts. The four phases are pre-window preparation (T-48h: staging deploy, baseline capture, rollback staging, on-call confirmation), deploy (rolling or canary at T+0 to T+30m), monitoring (60-minute golden window against baseline thresholds), and closure (unfreeze, update compliance record, file post-review ticket). The rollback plan is authored before the window opens: exact commands, trigger thresholds (2x error rate, 3x P99), and a time budget for rollback completion. Rolling deployment is the default for patch and minor bumps; blue-green for major version changes; canary for SDK upgrades where output quality is uncertain. Windows are scheduled on a biweekly recurring cadence, with an emergency slot for security patches that cannot wait for the next scheduled slot.
